<content>1: package taskstate
2: 
3: import (
4: 	"fmt"
5: 	"os"
6: 	"path/filepath"
7: 	"sort"
8: 	"strings"
9: 	"time"
10: 
11: 	"github.com/kastheco/kasmos/config/taskstore"
12: )
13: 
14: type Status string
15: 
16: const (
17: 	StatusReady     Status = "ready"
18: 	StatusDone      Status = "done"
19: 	StatusReviewing Status = "reviewing"
20: 	StatusCancelled Status = "cancelled"
21: 
22: 	// Lifecycle-stage statuses — canonical names used by the FSM.
23: 	StatusPlanning     Status = "planning"
24: 	StatusImplementing Status = "implementing"
25: )
26: 
27: type TaskEntry struct {
28: 	Status        Status    `json:"status"`
29: 	Description   string    `json:"description,omitempty"`
30: 	Branch        string    `json:"branch,omitempty"`
31: 	Topic         string    `json:"topic,omitempty"`
32: 	CreatedAt     time.Time `json:"created_at,omitempty"`
33: 	Implemented   string    `json:"implemented,omitempty"`
34: 	ClickUpTaskID string    `json:"clickup_task_id,omitempty"`
35: 	ReviewCycle   int       `json:"review_cycle,omitempty"`
36: }
37: 
38: type TopicEntry struct {
39: 	CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`
40: }
41: 
42: type TaskState struct {
43: 	Dir          string
44: 	Plans        map[string]TaskEntry
45: 	TopicEntries map[string]TopicEntry
46: 	store        taskstore.Store // always non-nil
47: 	project      string          // project name used with the store
48: }
49: 
50: type TaskInfo struct {
51: 	Filename    string
52: 	Status      Status
53: 	Description string
54: 	Branch      string
55: 	Topic       string
56: 	CreatedAt   time.Time
57: }
58: 
59: type TopicInfo struct {
60: 	Name      string
61: 	CreatedAt time.Time
62: }
63: 
64: // Load creates a TaskState backed by the given store. Plans and TopicEntries are
65: // populated from the store. dir is retained for file operations (e.g. Rename moves
66: // the .md file on disk). The store is always required — there is no JSON fallback.
67: func Load(store taskstore.Store, project, dir string) (*TaskState, error) {
68: 	plans, err := store.List(project)
69: 	if err != nil {
70: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
71: 	}
72: 
73: 	topics, err := store.ListTopics(project)
74: 	if err != nil {
75: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
76: 	}
77: 
78: 	ps := &TaskState{
79: 		Dir:          dir,
80: 		Plans:        make(map[string]TaskEntry, len(plans)),
81: 		TopicEntries: make(map[string]TopicEntry, len(topics)),
82: 		store:        store,
83: 		project:      project,
84: 	}
85: 
86: 	for _, e := range plans {
87: 		ps.Plans[e.Filename] = TaskEntry{
88: 			Status:        Status(e.Status),
89: 			Description:   e.Description,
90: 			Branch:        e.Branch,
91: 			Topic:         e.Topic,
92: 			CreatedAt:     e.CreatedAt,
93: 			Implemented:   e.Implemented,
94: 			ClickUpTaskID: e.ClickUpTaskID,
95: 			ReviewCycle:   e.ReviewCycle,
96: 		}
97: 	}
98: 
99: 	for _, t := range topics {
100: 		ps.TopicEntries[t.Name] = TopicEntry{CreatedAt: t.CreatedAt}
101: 	}
102: 
103: 	return ps, nil
104: }
105: 
106: // Topics returns all topic entries sorted by name.
107: func (ps *TaskState) Topics() []TopicInfo {
108: 	// Discover topics from both TopicEntries and plan topic fields.
109: 	seen := make(map[string]TopicInfo)
110: 	for name, entry := range ps.TopicEntries {
111: 		seen[name] = TopicInfo{Name: name, CreatedAt: entry.CreatedAt}
112: 	}
113: 	for _, entry := range ps.Plans {
114: 		if entry.Topic != "" {
115: 			if _, ok := seen[entry.Topic]; !ok {
116: 				seen[entry.Topic] = TopicInfo{Name: entry.Topic}
117: 			}
118: 		}
119: 	}
120: 	result := make([]TopicInfo, 0, len(seen))
121: 	for _, info := range seen {
122: 		result = append(result, info)
123: 	}
124: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
125: 		return result[i].Name < result[j].Name
126: 	})
127: 	return result
128: }
129: 
130: // TasksByTopic returns all plans in the given topic, sorted by filename.
131: func (ps *TaskState) TasksByTopic(topic string) []TaskInfo {
132: 	result := make([]TaskInfo, 0)
133: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
134: 		if entry.Topic == topic {
135: 			result = append(result, TaskInfo{
136: 				Filename: filename, Status: entry.Status,
137: 				Description: entry.Description, Branch: entry.Branch,
138: 				Topic: entry.Topic, CreatedAt: entry.CreatedAt,
139: 			})
140: 		}
141: 	}
142: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
143: 		return result[i].Filename < result[j].Filename
144: 	})
145: 	return result
146: }
147: 
148: // UngroupedTasks returns all active plans with no topic, sorted by filename.
149: func (ps *TaskState) UngroupedTasks() []TaskInfo {
150: 	result := make([]TaskInfo, 0)
151: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
152: 		if entry.Status == StatusDone || entry.Status == StatusCancelled {
153: 			continue
154: 		}
155: 		if entry.Topic == "" {
156: 			result = append(result, TaskInfo{
157: 				Filename: filename, Status: entry.Status,
158: 				Description: entry.Description, Branch: entry.Branch,
159: 				CreatedAt: entry.CreatedAt,
160: 			})
161: 		}
162: 	}
163: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
164: 		return result[i].Filename < result[j].Filename
165: 	})
166: 	return result
167: }
168: 
169: // hasTopicEntry returns true if the given topic name exists in TopicEntries.
170: func (ps *TaskState) hasTopicEntry(topic string) bool {
171: 	if ps.TopicEntries == nil {
172: 		return false
173: 	}
174: 	_, exists := ps.TopicEntries[topic]
175: 	return exists
176: }
177: 
178: // HasRunningCoderInTopic checks if any plan in the given topic (other than
179: // excludePlan) has status StatusImplementing. Returns the conflicting plan filename.
180: func (ps *TaskState) HasRunningCoderInTopic(topic, excludePlan string) (bool, string) {
181: 	if topic == "" {
182: 		return false, ""
183: 	}
184: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
185: 		if filename == excludePlan {
186: 			continue
187: 		}
188: 		if entry.Topic == topic && entry.Status == StatusImplementing {
189: 			return true, filename
190: 		}
191: 	}
192: 	return false, ""
193: }
194: 
195: // Unfinished returns plans that are not done or cancelled, sorted by filename.
196: func (ps *TaskState) Unfinished() []TaskInfo {
197: 	result := make([]TaskInfo, 0, len(ps.Plans))
198: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
199: 		if entry.Status == StatusDone || entry.Status == StatusCancelled {
200: 			continue
201: 		}
202: 		result = append(result, TaskInfo{
203: 			Filename: filename, Status: entry.Status,
204: 			Description: entry.Description, Branch: entry.Branch,
205: 			Topic: entry.Topic, CreatedAt: entry.CreatedAt,
206: 		})
207: 	}
208: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
209: 		return result[i].Filename < result[j].Filename
210: 	})
211: 	return result
212: }
213: 
214: // Finished returns plans that are done, sorted by creation time (newest first).
215: func (ps *TaskState) Finished() []TaskInfo {
216: 	result := make([]TaskInfo, 0)
217: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
218: 		if entry.Status != StatusDone {
219: 			continue
220: 		}
221: 		result = append(result, TaskInfo{
222: 			Filename: filename, Status: entry.Status,
223: 			Description: entry.Description, Branch: entry.Branch,
224: 			Topic: entry.Topic, CreatedAt: entry.CreatedAt,
225: 		})
226: 	}
227: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
228: 		if !result[i].CreatedAt.Equal(result[j].CreatedAt) {
229: 			return result[i].CreatedAt.After(result[j].CreatedAt)
230: 		}
231: 		return result[i].Filename > result[j].Filename
232: 	})
233: 	return result
234: }
235: 
236: // Cancelled returns all cancelled plans, sorted by filename.
237: func (ps *TaskState) Cancelled() []TaskInfo {
238: 	result := make([]TaskInfo, 0)
239: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
240: 		if entry.Status != StatusCancelled {
241: 			continue
242: 		}
243: 		result = append(result, TaskInfo{
244: 			Filename: filename, Status: entry.Status,
245: 			Description: entry.Description, Branch: entry.Branch,
246: 			Topic: entry.Topic, CreatedAt: entry.CreatedAt,
247: 		})
248: 	}
249: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
250: 		return result[i].Filename < result[j].Filename
251: 	})
252: 	return result
253: }
254: 
255: // List returns all plans (including done and cancelled), sorted by filename.
256: func (ps *TaskState) List() []TaskInfo {
257: 	result := make([]TaskInfo, 0, len(ps.Plans))
258: 	for filename, entry := range ps.Plans {
259: 		result = append(result, TaskInfo{
260: 			Filename:    filename,
261: 			Status:      entry.Status,
262: 			Description: entry.Description,
263: 			Branch:      entry.Branch,
264: 			Topic:       entry.Topic,
265: 			CreatedAt:   entry.CreatedAt,
266: 		})
267: 	}
268: 	sort.Slice(result, func(i, j int) bool {
269: 		return result[i].Filename < result[j].Filename
270: 	})
271: 	return result
272: }
273: 
274: // IsDone returns true only if the given plan has status StatusDone.
275: func (ps *TaskState) IsDone(filename string) bool {
276: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
277: 	if !ok {
278: 		return false
279: 	}
280: 	return entry.Status == StatusDone
281: }
282: 
283: // ForceSetStatus overrides a plan's status regardless of FSM rules.
284: // Validates the status is a known value. Use only for manual overrides (e.g. kq plan set-status --force).
285: func (ps *TaskState) ForceSetStatus(filename string, status Status) error {
286: 	if !isValidStatus(status) {
287: 		return fmt.Errorf("invalid status %q: must be one of ready, planning, implementing, reviewing, done, cancelled", status)
288: 	}
289: 	if _, ok := ps.Plans[filename]; !ok {
290: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", filename)
291: 	}
292: 	entry := ps.Plans[filename]
293: 	entry.Status = status
294: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
295: 	if err := ps.store.Update(ps.project, filename, ps.toTaskstoreEntry(filename, entry)); err != nil {
296: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
297: 	}
298: 	return nil
299: }
300: 
301: // isValidStatus returns true if s is a recognised lifecycle status.
302: func isValidStatus(s Status) bool {
303: 	switch s {
304: 	case StatusReady, StatusPlanning, StatusImplementing, StatusReviewing, StatusDone, StatusCancelled:
305: 		return true
306: 	}
307: 	return false
308: }
309: 
310: // setStatus updates a plan's status and persists to the store.
311: // Unexported: only for use within this package (tests). Production code must use taskfsm.Transition.
312: func (ps *TaskState) setStatus(filename string, status Status) error {
313: 	if ps.Plans == nil {
314: 		ps.Plans = make(map[string]TaskEntry)
315: 	}
316: 	entry := ps.Plans[filename]
317: 	entry.Status = status
318: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
319: 	if err := ps.store.Update(ps.project, filename, ps.toTaskstoreEntry(filename, entry)); err != nil {
320: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
321: 	}
322: 	return nil
323: }
324: 
325: // CreateWithContent adds a new plan entry with markdown content stored in the backend.
326: // The plan entry is created with StatusReady, and the content is persisted via store.SetContent.
327: // Returns an error if the plan already exists.
328: func (ps *TaskState) CreateWithContent(filename, description, branch, topic string, createdAt time.Time, content string) error {
329: 	if err := ps.Create(filename, description, branch, topic, createdAt); err != nil {
330: 		return err
331: 	}
332: 	if err := ps.store.SetContent(ps.project, filename, content); err != nil {
333: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store set content: %w", err)
334: 	}
335: 	return nil
336: }
337: 
338: // GetContent retrieves the markdown content for the given plan filename from the store.
339: func (ps *TaskState) GetContent(filename string) (string, error) {
340: 	return ps.store.GetContent(ps.project, filename)
341: }
342: 
343: // SetContent updates the markdown content for the given plan filename in the store.
344: func (ps *TaskState) SetContent(filename, content string) error {
345: 	return ps.store.SetContent(ps.project, filename, content)
346: }
347: 
348: // Create adds a new plan entry to the state and auto-creates the topic if needed.
349: func (ps *TaskState) Create(filename, description, branch, topic string, createdAt time.Time) error {
350: 	if ps.Plans == nil {
351: 		ps.Plans = make(map[string]TaskEntry)
352: 	}
353: 	if _, exists := ps.Plans[filename]; exists {
354: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan already exists: %s", filename)
355: 	}
356: 	entry := TaskEntry{
357: 		Status:      StatusReady,
358: 		Description: description,
359: 		Branch:      branch,
360: 		Topic:       topic,
361: 		CreatedAt:   createdAt.UTC(),
362: 	}
363: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
364: 	// Auto-create topic entry if it doesn't exist
365: 	if topic != "" {
366: 		if ps.TopicEntries == nil {
367: 			ps.TopicEntries = make(map[string]TopicEntry)
368: 		}
369: 		if _, exists := ps.TopicEntries[topic]; !exists {
370: 			ps.TopicEntries[topic] = TopicEntry{CreatedAt: createdAt.UTC()}
371: 		}
372: 	}
373: 	if err := ps.store.Create(ps.project, ps.toTaskstoreEntry(filename, entry)); err != nil {
374: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
375: 	}
376: 	// Auto-create topic in store if needed
377: 	if topic != "" {
378: 		topicEntry := taskstore.TopicEntry{Name: topic, CreatedAt: createdAt.UTC()}
379: 		if err := ps.store.CreateTopic(ps.project, topicEntry); err != nil {
380: 			// Ignore "already exists" errors for topics
381: 			if !isAlreadyExistsError(err) {
382: 				return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
383: 			}
384: 		}
385: 	}
386: 	return nil
387: }
388: 
389: // Register adds a new plan entry with metadata and persists to the store.
390: // Returns an error if the plan already exists.
391: func (ps *TaskState) Register(filename, description, branch string, createdAt time.Time) error {
392: 	if ps.Plans == nil {
393: 		ps.Plans = make(map[string]TaskEntry)
394: 	}
395: 	if _, exists := ps.Plans[filename]; exists {
396: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan already exists: %s", filename)
397: 	}
398: 	entry := TaskEntry{
399: 		Status:      StatusReady,
400: 		Description: description,
401: 		Branch:      branch,
402: 		CreatedAt:   createdAt.UTC(),
403: 	}
404: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
405: 	if err := ps.store.Create(ps.project, ps.toTaskstoreEntry(filename, entry)); err != nil {
406: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
407: 	}
408: 	return nil
409: }
410: 
411: // Entry returns the TaskEntry for the given filename, and whether it exists.
412: func (ps *TaskState) Entry(filename string) (TaskEntry, bool) {
413: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
414: 	return entry, ok
415: }
416: 
417: // SetTopic assigns a topic to an existing plan entry and persists to the store.
418: // If topic is non-empty and does not yet exist in TopicEntries, it is auto-created.
419: // Pass an empty string to remove the plan from any topic.
420: func (ps *TaskState) SetTopic(filename, topic string) error {
421: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
422: 	if !ok {
423: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", filename)
424: 	}
425: 	entry.Topic = topic
426: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
427: 	// Auto-create topic entry if it doesn't exist
428: 	if topic != "" {
429: 		if ps.TopicEntries == nil {
430: 			ps.TopicEntries = make(map[string]TopicEntry)
431: 		}
432: 		if _, exists := ps.TopicEntries[topic]; !exists {
433: 			ps.TopicEntries[topic] = TopicEntry{CreatedAt: time.Now().UTC()}
434: 		}
435: 	}
436: 	if err := ps.store.Update(ps.project, filename, ps.toTaskstoreEntry(filename, entry)); err != nil {
437: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
438: 	}
439: 	// Auto-create topic in store if needed
440: 	if topic != "" {
441: 		topicEntry := taskstore.TopicEntry{Name: topic, CreatedAt: ps.TopicEntries[topic].CreatedAt}
442: 		if err := ps.store.CreateTopic(ps.project, topicEntry); err != nil {
443: 			if !isAlreadyExistsError(err) {
444: 				return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
445: 			}
446: 		}
447: 	}
448: 	return nil
449: }
450: 
451: // SetBranch assigns a branch name to an existing plan entry and persists to the store.
452: func (ps *TaskState) SetBranch(filename, branch string) error {
453: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
454: 	if !ok {
455: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", filename)
456: 	}
457: 	entry.Branch = branch
458: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
459: 	if err := ps.store.Update(ps.project, filename, ps.toTaskstoreEntry(filename, entry)); err != nil {
460: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
461: 	}
462: 	return nil
463: }
464: 
465: // Save is a no-op — all mutations write through to the store immediately.
466: // Retained for API compatibility.
467: func (ps *TaskState) Save() error {
468: 	return nil
469: }
470: 
471: // DisplayName strips the .md extension from a plan filename.
472: // "auth-refactor.md" → "auth-refactor"
473: func DisplayName(filename string) string {
474: 	return strings.TrimSuffix(filename, ".md")
475: }
476: 
477: // Rename renames a plan by giving it a new display name slug.
478: // It renames the .md file on disk (if it exists), rekeys the planstate entry,
479: // and persists the updated state.
480: // newName should be a human-readable name (e.g., "auth refactor") which will be
481: // slugified automatically. Returns the new filename on success.
482: func (ps *TaskState) Rename(oldFilename, newName string) (string, error) {
483: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[oldFilename]
484: 	if !ok {
485: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", oldFilename)
486: 	}
487: 	if newName == "" {
488: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("new name cannot be empty")
489: 	}
490: 
491: 	// Build new filename from the slug alone.
492: 	newSlug := slugify(newName)
493: 	if newSlug == "" {
494: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("new name produced an empty slug")
495: 	}
496: 	newFilename := newSlug + ".md"
497: 
498: 	if newFilename == oldFilename {
499: 		return oldFilename, nil // nothing to do
500: 	}
501: 	if _, exists := ps.Plans[newFilename]; exists {
502: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("a plan named %q already exists", newFilename)
503: 	}
504: 
505: 	// Rename file on disk if it exists.
506: 	oldPath := filepath.Join(ps.Dir, oldFilename)
507: 	newPath := filepath.Join(ps.Dir, newFilename)
508: 	if _, err := os.Stat(oldPath); err == nil {
509: 		if err := os.Rename(oldPath, newPath); err != nil {
510: 			return "", fmt.Errorf("rename plan file: %w", err)
511: 		}
512: 	}
513: 
514: 	// Rekey the planstate entry.
515: 	ps.Plans[newFilename] = entry
516: 	delete(ps.Plans, oldFilename)
517: 
518: 	if err := ps.store.Rename(ps.project, oldFilename, newFilename); err != nil {
519: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
520: 	}
521: 	return newFilename, nil
522: }
523: 
524: // slugify converts a human name to a lowercase, hyphen-separated slug.
525: // "My Cool Feature!" → "my-cool-feature"
526: func slugify(name string) string {
527: 	name = strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(name))
528: 	// Replace any sequence of non-alphanumeric characters with a hyphen.
529: 	result := make([]rune, 0, len(name))
530: 	inHyphen := false
531: 	for _, r := range name {
532: 		if r >= 'a' && r <= 'z' || r >= '0' && r <= '9' {
533: 			result = append(result, r)
534: 			inHyphen = false
535: 		} else if !inHyphen && len(result) > 0 {
536: 			result = append(result, '-')
537: 			inHyphen = true
538: 		}
539: 	}
540: 	// Trim trailing hyphen.
541: 	for len(result) > 0 && result[len(result)-1] == '-' {
542: 		result = result[:len(result)-1]
543: 	}
544: 	return string(result)
545: }
546: 
547: // isAlreadyExistsError returns true if the error indicates a duplicate resource.
548: func isAlreadyExistsError(err error) bool {
549: 	if err == nil {
550: 		return false
551: 	}
552: 	msg := err.Error()
553: 	return strings.Contains(msg, "already exists")
554: }
555: 
556: // toTaskstoreEntry converts a local TaskEntry to a taskstore.TaskEntry for
557: // writing to the store.
558: func (ps *TaskState) toTaskstoreEntry(filename string, e TaskEntry) taskstore.TaskEntry {
559: 	return taskstore.TaskEntry{
560: 		Filename:      filename,
561: 		Status:        taskstore.Status(e.Status),
562: 		Description:   e.Description,
563: 		Branch:        e.Branch,
564: 		Topic:         e.Topic,
565: 		CreatedAt:     e.CreatedAt,
566: 		Implemented:   e.Implemented,
567: 		ClickUpTaskID: e.ClickUpTaskID,
568: 		ReviewCycle:   e.ReviewCycle,
569: 	}
570: }
571: 
572: // SetClickUpTaskID assigns a ClickUp task ID to an existing plan entry and
573: // persists to the store.
574: func (ps *TaskState) SetClickUpTaskID(filename, taskID string) error {
575: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
576: 	if !ok {
577: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", filename)
578: 	}
579: 	entry.ClickUpTaskID = taskID
580: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
581: 	if err := ps.store.SetClickUpTaskID(ps.project, filename, taskID); err != nil {
582: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
583: 	}
584: 	return nil
585: }
586: 
587: // ReviewCycle returns the current review cycle counter for the given plan.
588: // Returns an error if the plan is not found.
589: func (ps *TaskState) ReviewCycle(filename string) (int, error) {
590: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
591: 	if !ok {
592: 		return 0, fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", filename)
593: 	}
594: 	return entry.ReviewCycle, nil
595: }
596: 
597: // IncrementReviewCycle increments the review cycle counter for the given plan
598: // and persists to the store. Updates the in-memory state to reflect the new value.
599: // Returns an error if the plan is not found.
600: func (ps *TaskState) IncrementReviewCycle(filename string) error {
601: 	entry, ok := ps.Plans[filename]
602: 	if !ok {
603: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", filename)
604: 	}
605: 	if err := ps.store.IncrementReviewCycle(ps.project, filename); err != nil {
606: 		return fmt.Errorf("task store: %w", err)
607: 	}
608: 	entry.ReviewCycle++
609: 	ps.Plans[filename] = entry
610: 	return nil
611: }

(End of file - total 611 lines)
</content>

// tabBorderWithBottom constructs a rounded lipgloss border where the bottom
// edge uses the three supplied characters (left corner, fill, right corner).
func tabBorderWithBottom(left, middle, right string) lipgloss.Border {
	b := lipgloss.RoundedBorder()
	b.BottomLeft = left
	b.Bottom = middle
	b.BottomRight = right
	return b

parts.text:
I now have a thorough understanding of the codebase. Let me summarize what I've found:

1. **Current info pane** (`ui/info_pane.go`): Shows basic plan metadata (name, description, status, topic, branch, created) and instance metadata. For plan headers, it shows instance counts and a "view plan doc" button. It also shows wave progress for the current wave only.

2. **Task store** (`config/taskstore/`): SQLite-backed store with `TaskEntry` that has: filename, status, description, branch, topic, created_at, implemented, content, clickup_task_id, review_cycle. No subtask tracking in the DB.
   
3. **Task parser** (`config/taskparser/`): Parses plan markdown into a `Plan` struct containing goals, architecture, tech stack, and waves with their tasks.

4. **Task state** (`config/taskstate/`): In-memory cache wrapping the task store with helper methods, but doesn't track subtask statuses.

5. **Wave orchestrator** (`orchestration/engine.go`): Manages task lifecycle states in memory only—no persistence to the database.

6. **App layer** (`app/app_`): The thinking cuts off here, but this is likely where the UI connects to the orchestration logic.

So the core issue is that task completion states live only in memory and vanish on restart. The info pane is also pretty bare—it only shows instance counts and current wave progress, missing the plan summary, full wave breakdown, individual task details, overall progress metrics, and any historical tracking. Plus there's no structured way to store subtask data in the database itself.

The user wants me to persist plan metadata in the database and redesign the info tab to display the plan summary, detailed implementation progress across all waves and tasks with their statuses, and dev lifecycle events. Before jumping into code, they want me to brainstorm and create mockups.

I'm thinking about adding a new `subtasks` table to the SQLite schema that tracks each task by plan filename, wave number, task number, title, status, and timestamp. When a plan gets registered or updated, I'd parse the content to extract all tasks and populate the subtask rows accordingly. The user explicitly wants DB persistence and lifecycle tracking, so I need to move forward with presenting design options rather than asking preliminary questions. Let me lay out the three approaches with their tradeoffs so we can explore mockups together. I'm picturing the info pane redesign with a plan summary at the top showing the goal and tech stack, then an all-waves progress view with task lists and status indicators for each wave, plus a lifecycle timeline tracking the plan from creation through implementation to completion. This is a medium-to-large feature, so I should clarify the scope before diving into the full implementation plan.

I now have a thorough understanding of the architecture. Let me start the design exploration phase.

## Current State Summary

**What exists:**
- `TaskEntry` in the DB: filename, status, description, branch, topic, created_at, content — but **no subtask/wave tracking**
- `WaveOrchestrator`: tracks task states (pending/running/complete/failed) **in-memory only** — lost on TUI restart
- `taskparser.Parse()`: extracts `Plan{Goal, Architecture, TechStack, []Wave{[]Task}}` from markdown content
- Info pane: shows basic plan metadata + instance counts + current wave progress only

**Key gaps:**
1. **No subtask persistence** — task completion states are ephemeral
2. **Info pane is shallow** — shows instance counts and the current wave's task glyphs but no plan summary, no cross-wave progress, no lifecycle timeline
3. **No lifecycle timestamps** — we know `created_at` but not when planning/implementation/review started

Before I draft the full plan, I have a design question:

**Should we add a lifecycle timeline to the info pane?** The DB currently stores `created_at` but not individual phase timestamps (e.g. "planning started", "implementation started at", "review started at"). Two options:

- **A (recommended): Add phase timestamps to the `tasks` table** — new columns like `planning_at`, `implementing_at`, `reviewing_at`, `done_at`. Simple, queryable, low overhead. The FSM transition points already exist — we just need to record the timestamp when each transition fires.

- **B: Separate `plan_events` table** — a full event log (plan_file, event_type, timestamp, detail). More flexible but heavier; we already have the audit log for detailed event history.

I lean toward **A** — phase timestamps on the existing table give us the lifecycle view without a new table. The audit log covers detailed event history if needed. Does that work, or do you want the full event log approach?

# Fix Agents Reading From Disk Implementation Plan

**Goal:** Stop agents from reading plan content via disk files at `docs/plans/`. Add a `kas task show` CLI command so agents can retrieve plan content from the database, and update all prompt builders, review templates, and skill documentation to reference the CLI instead of disk paths.

**Architecture:** Three parallel changes: (1) Add a `kas task show <plan-file>` subcommand to `cmd/task.go` that reads content from the task store and prints raw markdown to stdout — follows the existing `resolvePlansDir()` → `resolveStore()` pattern. (2) Update all prompt-building functions in `app/app_state.go`, `orchestration/prompt.go`, and `app/app_actions.go` to reference `kas task show` instead of `docs/plans/` — also update `review-prompt.md` template and corresponding test assertions. (3) Update skill documentation (scaffold templates + live copies) to document `kas task show` as the standard way agents retrieve plan content.

**Tech Stack:** Go, cobra (CLI), config/taskstore, config/taskstate, testify, `sd` for batch markdown edits

**Size:** Small (estimated ~2 hours, 3 tasks, 1 wave)

---

## Wave 1: Add CLI command and update all agent-facing references

### Task 1: Add `kas task show` CLI subcommand

**Files:**
- Modify: `cmd/task.go`
- Test: `cmd/task_test.go`

**Step 1: write the failing test**

Add a test in `cmd/task_test.go` that calls `executeTaskShow` and verifies it returns the stored plan content:

```go
func TestExecuteTaskShow(t *testing.T) {
	dir := t.TempDir()
	plansDir := filepath.Join(dir, "docs", "plans")
	require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(plansDir, 0o755))

	store := taskstore.NewTestSQLiteStore(t)
	project := projectFromPlansDir(plansDir)
	require.NoError(t, store.Create(project, taskstore.TaskEntry{
		Filename: "my-plan.md",
		Status:   taskstore.StatusReady,
		Content:  "# My Plan\n\n## Wave 1\n\n### Task 1: Do it\n\nDo the thing.\n",
	}))

	content, err := executeTaskShow(plansDir, "my-plan.md", store)
	require.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, "# My Plan\n\n## Wave 1\n\n### Task 1: Do it\n\nDo the thing.\n", content)
}

func TestExecuteTaskShow_NotFound(t *testing.T) {
	dir := t.TempDir()
	plansDir := filepath.Join(dir, "docs", "plans")
	require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(plansDir, 0o755))

	store := taskstore.NewTestSQLiteStore(t)
	_, err := executeTaskShow(plansDir, "nonexistent.md", store)
	require.Error(t, err)
	assert.Contains(t, err.Error(), "not found")
}

func TestExecuteTaskShow_EmptyContent(t *testing.T) {
	dir := t.TempDir()
	plansDir := filepath.Join(dir, "docs", "plans")
	require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(plansDir, 0o755))

	store := taskstore.NewTestSQLiteStore(t)
	project := projectFromPlansDir(plansDir)
	require.NoError(t, store.Create(project, taskstore.TaskEntry{
		Filename: "empty.md",
		Status:   taskstore.StatusReady,
	}))

	_, err := executeTaskShow(plansDir, "empty.md", store)
	require.Error(t, err)
	assert.Contains(t, err.Error(), "no content")
}
```

**Step 2: run test to verify it fails**

```bash
go test ./cmd/... -run "TestExecuteTaskShow" -v
```

expected: FAIL — `executeTaskShow` is undefined

**Step 3: write minimal implementation**

Add `executeTaskShow` function and the `show` cobra subcommand in `cmd/task.go`:

```go
// executeTaskShow retrieves plan content from the task store and returns it
// as raw markdown. Returns an error if the plan doesn't exist or has no content.
func executeTaskShow(plansDir, planFile string, store taskstore.Store) (string, error) {
	ps, err := loadTaskState(plansDir, store)
	if err != nil {
		return "", err
	}
	if _, ok := ps.Entry(planFile); !ok {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("task not found: %s", planFile)
	}
	content, err := ps.GetContent(planFile)
	if err != nil {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("get content for %s: %w", planFile, err)
	}
	if strings.TrimSpace(content) == "" {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("no content stored for %s", planFile)
	}
	return content, nil
}
```

Add the cobra subcommand inside `NewTaskCmd()`, alongside the existing subcommands:

```go
// kq task show
showCmd := &cobra.Command{
	Use:   "show <plan-file>",
	Short: "print plan content from the task store",
	Args:  cobra.ExactArgs(1),
	RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
		plansDir, err := resolvePlansDir()
		if err != nil {
			return err
		}
		content, err := executeTaskShow(plansDir, args[0], resolveStore(plansDir))
		if err != nil {
			return err
		}
		fmt.Print(content)
		return nil
	},
}
planCmd.AddCommand(showCmd)
```

**Step 4: run test to verify it passes**

```bash
go test ./cmd/... -run "TestExecuteTaskShow" -v
```

expected: PASS

**Step 5: commit**

```bash
git add cmd/task.go cmd/task_test.go
git commit -m "feat(task-1): add kas task show subcommand for CLI-based plan content retrieval"
```

### Task 2: Update prompt builders and review template to reference `kas task show`

**Files:**
- Modify: `app/app_state.go`
- Modify: `app/app_actions.go`
- Modify: `orchestration/prompt.go`
- Modify: `internal/initcmd/scaffold/templates/shared/review-prompt.md`
- Test: `app/app_task_actions_test.go`
- Test: `orchestration/prompt_test.go`

**Step 1: write the failing test**

Update existing tests in `app/app_task_actions_test.go` to assert prompts reference `kas task show` instead of `docs/plans/`:

```go
// TestBuildImplementPrompt — assert prompt says "kas task show" not "docs/plans/"
func TestBuildImplementPrompt(t *testing.T) {
	prompt := buildImplementPrompt("auth-refactor.md")
	assert.Contains(t, prompt, "kas task show auth-refactor.md")
	assert.NotContains(t, prompt, "docs/plans/")
}

// TestBuildSoloPrompt_WithDescription — assert prompt says "kas task show" not "docs/plans/"
func TestBuildSoloPrompt_WithDescription(t *testing.T) {
	prompt := buildSoloPrompt("auth-refactor", "Refactor JWT auth", "auth-refactor.md")
	assert.Contains(t, prompt, "kas task show auth-refactor.md")
	assert.NotContains(t, prompt, "docs/plans/")
}

// TestBuildSoloPrompt_StubOnly — no plan file, no kas task show reference
func TestBuildSoloPrompt_StubOnly(t *testing.T) {
	prompt := buildSoloPrompt("quick-fix", "Fix the login bug", "")
	assert.NotContains(t, prompt, "kas task show")
	assert.NotContains(t, prompt, "docs/plans/")
}
```

Update `orchestration/prompt_test.go` for `BuildWaveAnnotationPrompt`:

```go
func TestBuildWaveAnnotationPrompt(t *testing.T) {
	prompt := BuildWaveAnnotationPrompt("my-feature.md")
	assert.Contains(t, prompt, "kas task show my-feature.md")
	assert.Contains(t, prompt, "## Wave")
	assert.Contains(t, prompt, "planner-finished-my-feature.md")
	assert.NotContains(t, prompt, "The plan at docs/plans/")
}
```

**Step 2: run test to verify it fails**

```bash
go test ./app/... -run "TestBuildImplementPrompt|TestBuildSoloPrompt" -v
go test ./orchestration/... -run "TestBuildWaveAnnotationPrompt" -v
```

expected: FAIL — prompts still reference `docs/plans/`

**Step 3: write minimal implementation**

**3a. Update `app/app_state.go`** — modify these functions:

`buildImplementPrompt`:
```go
func buildImplementPrompt(planFile string) string {
	return fmt.Sprintf(
		"Implement %s using the `kasmos-coder` skill. "+
			"Retrieve the full plan with `kas task show %s` and execute all tasks sequentially.",
		planFile, planFile,
	)
}
```

`buildSoloPrompt`:
```go
func buildSoloPrompt(planName, description, planFile string) string {
	if planFile != "" {
		return fmt.Sprintf(
			"Implement %s. Goal: %s. Retrieve the full plan with `kas task show %s`.",
			planName, description, planFile,
		)
	}
	return fmt.Sprintf("Implement %s. Goal: %s.", planName, description)
}
```

`buildModifyTaskPrompt`:
```go
func buildModifyTaskPrompt(planFile string) string {
	return fmt.Sprintf(
		"Modify existing plan %s. Retrieve current content with `kas task show %s`. "+
			"Keep the same filename and update only what changed.",
		planFile, planFile,
	)
}
```

`buildWaveAnnotationPrompt` in `app/app_state.go`:
```go
func buildWaveAnnotationPrompt(planFile string) string {
	return fmt.Sprintf(
		"The plan %[1]s is missing ## Wave N headers required for kasmos wave orchestration. "+
			"Retrieve the plan content with `kas task show %[1]s`, then annotate it by wrapping "+
			"all tasks under ## Wave N sections. "+
			"Every plan needs at least ## Wave 1 — even single-task trivial plans. "+
			"Keep all existing task content intact; only add the ## Wave headers.\n\n"+
			"After annotating:\n"+
			"1. Write the updated plan to docs/plans/%[1]s\n"+
			"2. Commit: git add docs/plans/%[1]s && git commit -m \"plan: add wave headers to %[1]s\"\n"+
			"3. Signal completion: touch .kasmos/signals/planner-finished-%[1]s\n"+
			"Do not edit plan-state.json directly.",
		planFile,
	)
}
```

Note: The annotation prompt still references `docs/plans/` for the *write* path (commit step). The plan is read via CLI, written to disk for git commit, and the TUI ingests the committed content. This is correct — the goal is to stop agents from *reading* from disk, not from *writing* for commits.

`buildChatAboutTaskPrompt`:
```go
func buildChatAboutTaskPrompt(planFile string, entry taskstate.TaskEntry, question string) string {
	name := taskstate.DisplayName(planFile)
	var sb strings.Builder
	sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("You are answering a question about the plan '%s'.\n\n", name))
	sb.WriteString("## Plan Context\n\n")
	sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("- **Plan:** %s\n", planFile))
	sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("- **Status:** %s\n", entry.Status))
	if entry.Description != "" {
		sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("- **Description:** %s\n", entry.Description))
	}
	if entry.Branch != "" {
		sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("- **Branch:** %s\n", entry.Branch))
	}
	if entry.Topic != "" {
		sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("- **Topic:** %s\n", entry.Topic))
	}
	sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("\nRetrieve the full plan with `kas task show %s` for details.\n\n", planFile))
	sb.WriteString("## User Question\n\n")
	sb.WriteString(question)
	return sb.String()
}
```

The user chose option A — phase timestamps on the existing `tasks` table. Now let me continue the design exploration with mockups before writing the implementation plan.

Let me think about what the improved info pane should look like. I need to design TUI mockups showing the new layout.

Let me think about the info pane layout for the two views:

1. **Plan header selected** (no instance) — the main view we're improving
2. **Instance selected** (with plan) — also gets improvements

For the plan header view, I want to show:
- Plan summary (goal, architecture, tech stack extracted from content)
- Status with lifecycle timeline


- Progress overview across all waves and tasks, instance counts, and review cycle information

Now I'm drafting ASCII mockups to present to the user for feedback.

Building the plan card layout... I'm realizing the info pane sits within the tabbed window rather than standing alone, so I should strip away the extra borders and focus on the actual content structure. Before diving into the full implementation plan, I want to show the mockups and get some feedback on the direction.

Good — phase timestamps on the existing table it is. Now let me draft TUI mockups for the improved info pane before writing the implementation plan.

Here are two mockup options for when a **plan header** is selected (the main view we're overhauling):

---

**Mockup A: Dense vertical layout (recommended)**

```
plan
──────────────────────────────────────
name            improved-info-tab
status          implementing
branch          plan/improved-info-tab
review cycle    2

goal
──────────────────────────────────────
store plan metadata and subtask statuses
in the database, then redesign the info
tab to track the whole dev lifecycle

lifecycle
──────────────────────────────────────
● created        2026-03-01 09:00
● planning       2026-03-01 09:05
● implementing   2026-03-01 10:30
○ reviewing      —
○ done           —

progress                  ████████░░ 4/6
──────────────────────────────────────
wave 1 ✓
  ✓ task 1  subtask table schema
  ✓ task 2  store interface methods
wave 2 ●
  ✓ task 3  http endpoints
  ● task 4  info pane UI overhaul
  ○ task 5  app layer wiring
wave 3 ○
  ○ task 6  integration tests

instances       3 (2 running, 1 ready)
+23 -4 lines changed

               [ view plan doc ]
```

**Mockup B: Compact with inline progress**

```
plan
──────────────────────────────────────
name            improved-info-tab
status          implementing
branch          plan/improved-info-tab
goal            store plan metadata and
                subtask statuses in the db

timeline        created → planning →
                implementing (now)

progress        ████████░░ 4/6 (67%)
  w1 ✓✓  w2 ✓●○  w3 ○

instances       3 (2 running, 1 ready)
+23 -4 lines changed

               [ view plan doc ]
```

---

**My recommendation: Mockup A.** It's more informative — the lifecycle section clearly shows which phases have happened and when, the per-task breakdown lets you see exactly what's done/running/pending, and it scrolls naturally in the existing viewport. Mockup B is more compact but loses the task-level detail which is the main value-add.

For the **instance-selected** view, I'd keep the existing layout but add the plan goal as a one-liner below the plan name, and show the instance's specific task assignment (e.g. "task 4 of 6: info pane UI overhaul").

Which mockup direction? And any sections you'd add/remove/change?

diffs.after:
# Improved Info Tab Implementation Plan

**Goal:** Persist plan subtask statuses and phase timestamps in the database, then overhaul the info pane UI to show plan summary, lifecycle timeline, and all-waves progress with per-task detail.

**Architecture:** Add a `subtasks` table and phase-timestamp columns to the existing SQLite task store. Parse plan content during ingestion to populate subtask rows and extract the goal field. Hook FSM transitions to record phase timestamps and orchestrator state changes to update subtask status. Redesign the info pane to render the new data in a scrollable multi-section layout (metadata → goal → lifecycle → progress → instances).

**Tech Stack:** Go, SQLite (modernc.org/sqlite), bubbletea/lipgloss (charm v2), existing taskstore/taskstate/taskfsm packages.

**Size:** Medium (estimated ~2.5 hours, 4 tasks, 3 waves)

---

## Wave 1: Database Schema and SQLite Store

### Task 1: Subtask Table, Phase Timestamps, and SQLiteStore Methods

**Files:**
- Modify: `config/taskstore/store.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstore/sqlite.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstore/testing.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstore/sqlite_test.go`

**Step 1: write the failing test**

Add tests to `sqlite_test.go` that exercise the new subtask and phase-timestamp methods:

```go
func TestSQLiteStore_SubtaskCRUD(t *testing.T) {
    store := newTestStore(t)
    project := "test-project"

    // Create a plan first.
    store.Create(project, TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: StatusReady})

    // SetSubtasks should insert subtask rows.
    subtasks := []SubtaskEntry{
        {PlanFilename: "plan.md", WaveNumber: 1, TaskNumber: 1, Title: "schema migration", Status: SubtaskPending},
        {PlanFilename: "plan.md", WaveNumber: 1, TaskNumber: 2, Title: "store methods", Status: SubtaskPending},
        {PlanFilename: "plan.md", WaveNumber: 2, TaskNumber: 3, Title: "UI overhaul", Status: SubtaskPending},
    }
    require.NoError(t, store.SetSubtasks(project, "plan.md", subtasks))

    // GetSubtasks should return them ordered by task_number.
    got, err := store.GetSubtasks(project, "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    require.Len(t, got, 3)
    assert.Equal(t, "schema migration", got[0].Title)
    assert.Equal(t, SubtaskPending, got[0].Status)
    assert.Equal(t, 1, got[0].WaveNumber)

    // UpdateSubtaskStatus should change a single subtask's status.
    require.NoError(t, store.UpdateSubtaskStatus(project, "plan.md", 1, SubtaskComplete))
    got, _ = store.GetSubtasks(project, "plan.md")
    assert.Equal(t, SubtaskComplete, got[0].Status)

    // SetSubtasks again should replace (upsert) all rows.
    require.NoError(t, store.SetSubtasks(project, "plan.md", subtasks[:1]))
    got, _ = store.GetSubtasks(project, "plan.md")
    assert.Len(t, got, 1)
}

func TestSQLiteStore_PhaseTimestamps(t *testing.T) {
    store := newTestStore(t)
    project := "test-project"
    now := time.Now().UTC().Truncate(time.Millisecond)

    store.Create(project, TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: StatusReady})
    require.NoError(t, store.SetPhaseTimestamp(project, "plan.md", "implementing", now))

    entry, err := store.Get(project, "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    assert.Equal(t, now.Unix(), entry.ImplementingAt.Unix())
}

func TestSQLiteStore_PlanGoal(t *testing.T) {
    store := newTestStore(t)
    project := "test-project"

    store.Create(project, TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: StatusReady})
    require.NoError(t, store.SetPlanGoal(project, "plan.md", "add dark mode"))

    entry, err := store.Get(project, "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    assert.Equal(t, "add dark mode", entry.Goal)
}
```

**Step 2: run test to verify it fails**

```bash
go test ./config/taskstore/... -run 'TestSQLiteStore_Subtask|TestSQLiteStore_Phase|TestSQLiteStore_PlanGoal' -v
```

expected: FAIL — `SubtaskEntry` undefined, `SetSubtasks` undefined, etc.

**Step 3: write minimal implementation**

In `store.go`, add the subtask types and new Store interface methods:

```go
type SubtaskStatus string

const (
    SubtaskPending  SubtaskStatus = "pending"
    SubtaskRunning  SubtaskStatus = "running"
    SubtaskComplete SubtaskStatus = "complete"
    SubtaskFailed   SubtaskStatus = "failed"
)

type SubtaskEntry struct {
    PlanFilename string        `json:"plan_filename"`
    WaveNumber   int           `json:"wave_number"`
    TaskNumber   int           `json:"task_number"`
    Title        string        `json:"title"`
    Status       SubtaskStatus `json:"status"`
    UpdatedAt    time.Time     `json:"updated_at,omitempty"`
}
```

Add phase-timestamp fields to `TaskEntry`:

```go
PlanningAt     time.Time `json:"planning_at,omitempty"`
ImplementingAt time.Time `json:"implementing_at,omitempty"`
ReviewingAt    time.Time `json:"reviewing_at,omitempty"`
DoneAt         time.Time `json:"done_at,omitempty"`
Goal           string    `json:"goal,omitempty"`
```

Add new methods to the `Store` interface:

```go
// Subtask operations
SetSubtasks(project, planFilename string, subtasks []SubtaskEntry) error
GetSubtasks(project, planFilename string) ([]SubtaskEntry, error)
UpdateSubtaskStatus(project, planFilename string, taskNumber int, status SubtaskStatus) error

// Phase timestamps
SetPhaseTimestamp(project, planFilename, phase string, ts time.Time) error

// Plan metadata
SetPlanGoal(project, planFilename, goal string) error
```

In `sqlite.go`:
- Add the `subtasks` table to the schema constant.
- Add migrations: `subtasksTableMigration`, plus `ALTER TABLE tasks ADD COLUMN` for `planning_at`, `implementing_at`, `reviewing_at`, `done_at`, `goal`.
- Run migrations in `NewSQLiteStore`.
- Update `scanTaskEntry`/`scanTaskEntries` to include the 5 new columns.
- Update `Create`/`Update`/`Get`/`List`/`ListByStatus`/`ListByTopic` queries to include the new columns.
- Implement `SetSubtasks` (DELETE existing + INSERT new in a transaction).
- Implement `GetSubtasks` (SELECT ordered by task_number).
- Implement `UpdateSubtaskStatus` (UPDATE single row).
- Implement `SetPhaseTimestamp` (UPDATE the appropriate column by phase name).
- Implement `SetPlanGoal` (UPDATE goal column).

**Step 4: run test to verify it passes**

```bash
go test ./config/taskstore/... -run 'TestSQLiteStore_Subtask|TestSQLiteStore_Phase|TestSQLiteStore_PlanGoal' -v
```

expected: PASS

**Step 5: commit**

```bash
git add config/taskstore/store.go config/taskstore/sqlite.go config/taskstore/sqlite_test.go config/taskstore/testing.go
git commit -m "feat: add subtasks table, phase timestamps, and goal column to task store"
```

## Wave 2: HTTP Transport and Data Ingestion Pipeline

> **depends on wave 1:** HTTPStore and server need the Store interface additions from Task 1. Data ingestion needs the SetSubtasks/SetPhaseTimestamp/SetPlanGoal methods.

### Task 2: HTTPStore Client and Server Endpoints

**Files:**
- Modify: `config/taskstore/http.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstore/server.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstore/http_test.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstore/server_test.go`

**Step 1: write the failing test**

Add round-trip tests in `http_test.go` using the test server pattern already established in the file:

```go
func TestHTTPStore_SubtaskRoundTrip(t *testing.T) {
    // Setup: in-memory SQLite behind HTTP server.
    sqlite := newTestStore(t).(*SQLiteStore)
    srv := httptest.NewServer(NewHandler(sqlite))
    defer srv.Close()
    client := NewHTTPStore(srv.URL, "proj")

    sqlite.Create("proj", TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: StatusReady})

    subtasks := []SubtaskEntry{
        {PlanFilename: "plan.md", WaveNumber: 1, TaskNumber: 1, Title: "task one", Status: SubtaskPending},
        {PlanFilename: "plan.md", WaveNumber: 2, TaskNumber: 2, Title: "task two", Status: SubtaskPending},
    }
    require.NoError(t, client.SetSubtasks("proj", "plan.md", subtasks))

    got, err := client.GetSubtasks("proj", "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    require.Len(t, got, 2)

    require.NoError(t, client.UpdateSubtaskStatus("proj", "plan.md", 1, SubtaskComplete))
    got, _ = client.GetSubtasks("proj", "plan.md")
    assert.Equal(t, SubtaskComplete, got[0].Status)
}

func TestHTTPStore_PhaseTimestampRoundTrip(t *testing.T) {
    sqlite := newTestStore(t).(*SQLiteStore)
    srv := httptest.NewServer(NewHandler(sqlite))
    defer srv.Close()
    client := NewHTTPStore(srv.URL, "proj")
    now := time.Now().UTC().Truncate(time.Millisecond)

    sqlite.Create("proj", TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: StatusReady})

    require.NoError(t, client.SetPhaseTimestamp("proj", "plan.md", "implementing", now))
    entry, err := client.Get("proj", "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    assert.Equal(t, now.Unix(), entry.ImplementingAt.Unix())
}

func TestHTTPStore_PlanGoalRoundTrip(t *testing.T) {
    sqlite := newTestStore(t).(*SQLiteStore)
    srv := httptest.NewServer(NewHandler(sqlite))
    defer srv.Close()
    client := NewHTTPStore(srv.URL, "proj")

    sqlite.Create("proj", TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: StatusReady})

    require.NoError(t, client.SetPlanGoal("proj", "plan.md", "add dark mode"))
    entry, err := client.Get("proj", "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    assert.Equal(t, "add dark mode", entry.Goal)
}
```

**Step 2: run test to verify it fails**

```bash
go test ./config/taskstore/... -run 'TestHTTPStore_Subtask|TestHTTPStore_Phase|TestHTTPStore_PlanGoal' -v
```

expected: FAIL — `SetSubtasks` not implemented on HTTPStore

**Step 3: write minimal implementation**

In `server.go`, add 5 new endpoints mirroring existing patterns:
- `GET /v1/projects/{project}/tasks/{filename}/subtasks` → GetSubtasks
- `PUT /v1/projects/{project}/tasks/{filename}/subtasks` → SetSubtasks
- `PUT /v1/projects/{project}/tasks/{filename}/subtasks/{taskNumber}/status` → UpdateSubtaskStatus
- `PUT /v1/projects/{project}/tasks/{filename}/phase-timestamp` → SetPhaseTimestamp
- `PUT /v1/projects/{project}/tasks/{filename}/goal` → SetPlanGoal

In `http.go`, implement the corresponding HTTPStore methods using the same `do()`/`decodeError()` pattern as existing methods. Each method builds a URL, marshals JSON, sends the request, and checks the response status.

**Step 4: run test to verify it passes**

```bash
go test ./config/taskstore/... -run 'TestHTTPStore_Subtask|TestHTTPStore_Phase|TestHTTPStore_PlanGoal' -v
```

expected: PASS

**Step 5: commit**

```bash
git add config/taskstore/http.go config/taskstore/server.go config/taskstore/http_test.go config/taskstore/server_test.go
git commit -m "feat: add HTTP transport for subtasks, phase timestamps, and plan goal"
```

### Task 3: Subtask Ingestion, Phase Timestamps, and Status Persistence

**Files:**
- Modify: `config/taskstate/taskstate.go`
- Modify: `config/taskstate/taskstate_test.go`
- Modify: `config/taskfsm/fsm.go`
- Modify: `config/taskfsm/fsm_test.go`
- Modify: `app/wave_orchestrator.go`
- Modify: `app/wave_orchestrator_test.go`

**Step 1: write the failing test**

In `config/taskstate/taskstate_test.go`:

```go
func TestTaskState_IngestSubtasks(t *testing.T) {
    store := taskstore.NewTestSQLiteStore(t)
    ps, err := taskstate.Load(store, "proj", t.TempDir())
    require.NoError(t, err)

    require.NoError(t, ps.Create("plan.md", "desc", "plan/test", "", time.Now()))

    content := "# Plan\n\n**Goal:** add dark mode\n\n## Wave 1\n\n### Task 1: Schema\n\nbody\n\n### Task 2: Store\n\nbody\n\n## Wave 2\n\n### Task 3: UI\n\nbody\n"
    require.NoError(t, ps.IngestContent("plan.md", content))

    // Goal should be extracted.
    entry, _ := ps.Entry("plan.md")
    assert.Equal(t, "add dark mode", entry.Goal)

    // Subtasks should be created.
    subtasks, err := ps.GetSubtasks("plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    require.Len(t, subtasks, 3)
    assert.Equal(t, "Schema", subtasks[0].Title)
    assert.Equal(t, 1, subtasks[0].WaveNumber)
}
```

In `config/taskfsm/fsm_test.go`:

```go
func TestFSM_TransitionRecordsPhaseTimestamp(t *testing.T) {
    store := taskstore.NewTestSQLiteStore(t)
    store.Create("proj", taskstore.TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: taskstore.StatusReady})
    fsm := New(store, "proj", t.TempDir())

    require.NoError(t, fsm.Transition("plan.md", ImplementStart))

    entry, err := store.Get("proj", "plan.md")
    require.NoError(t, err)
    assert.False(t, entry.ImplementingAt.IsZero(), "implementing_at should be set")
}
```

In `app/wave_orchestrator_test.go`, add a test for subtask status persistence:

```go
func TestWaveOrchestrator_PersistsSubtaskStatus(t *testing.T) {
    store := taskstore.NewTestSQLiteStore(t)
    store.Create("proj", taskstore.TaskEntry{Filename: "plan.md", Status: taskstore.StatusImplementing})
    plan := &taskparser.Plan{Waves: []taskparser.Wave{
        {Number: 1, Tasks: []taskparser.Task{{Number: 1, Title: "first"}}},
    }}
    orch := NewWaveOrchestrator("plan.md", plan)
    orch.SetStore(store, "proj")
    orch.StartNextWave()
    orch.MarkTaskComplete(1)

    subtasks, _ := store.GetSubtasks("proj", "plan.md")
    require.Len(t, subtasks, 1)
    assert.Equal(t, taskstore.SubtaskComplete, subtasks[0].Status)
}
```

**Step 2: run test to verify it fails**

```bash
go test ./config/taskstate/... -run TestTaskState_IngestSubtasks -v
go test ./config/taskfsm/... -run TestFSM_TransitionRecordsPhaseTimestamp -v
go test ./app/... -run TestWaveOrchestrator_PersistsSubtaskStatus -v
```

expected: FAIL — `IngestContent`, `GetSubtasks`, `SetStore` undefined

**Step 3: write minimal implementation**

**taskstate.go** additions:
- Add `Goal` field to `taskstate.TaskEntry`, and phase timestamp fields: `PlanningAt`, `ImplementingAt`, `ReviewingAt`, `DoneAt`.
- Update `Load()` to map new fields from `taskstore.TaskEntry`.
- Update `toTaskstoreEntry()` to include new fields.
- Add `IngestContent(filename, content string) error` — calls `taskparser.Parse()`, extracts goal, calls `store.SetPlanGoal()`, builds `[]SubtaskEntry` from parsed waves/tasks, calls `store.SetSubtasks()`, then calls `store.SetContent()`.
- Add `GetSubtasks(filename string) ([]taskstore.SubtaskEntry, error)` — delegates to store.
- Add `UpdateSubtaskStatus(filename string, taskNumber int, status taskstore.SubtaskStatus) error` — delegates to store.

**fsm.go** additions:
- After `ForceSetStatus()` succeeds in `Transition()`, call `store.SetPhaseTimestamp()` with the new status name and `time.Now().UTC()`. Only set for recognized phases: "planning", "implementing", "reviewing", "done".

**wave_orchestrator.go** additions:
- Add optional `store taskstore.Store` and `project string` fields.
- Add `SetStore(store taskstore.Store, project string)` method.
- In `MarkTaskComplete()` and `MarkTaskFailed()`, if `store` is non-nil, call `store.UpdateSubtaskStatus()` with the appropriate status.
- In `StartNextWave()`, if `store` is non-nil, call `store.UpdateSubtaskStatus()` for each task being started (set to `SubtaskRunning`).

**Step 4: run test to verify it passes**

```bash
go test ./config/taskstate/... -run TestTaskState_IngestSubtasks -v
go test ./config/taskfsm/... -run TestFSM_TransitionRecordsPhaseTimestamp -v
go test ./app/... -run TestWaveOrchestrator_PersistsSubtaskStatus -v
```

expected: PASS

**Step 5: commit**

```bash
git add config/taskstate/taskstate.go config/taskstate/taskstate_test.go config/taskfsm/fsm.go config/taskfsm/fsm_test.go app/wave_orchestrator.go app/wave_orchestrator_test.go
git commit -m "feat: wire subtask ingestion, phase timestamps, and status persistence"
```

## Wave 3: Info Pane UI Overhaul

> **depends on wave 2:** The info pane reads subtask data and phase timestamps populated by the ingestion pipeline (Task 3). The app layer wiring calls store methods added in Tasks 1-2.

### Task 4: Redesign Info Pane with Lifecycle, Progress, and Goal Sections

**Files:**
- Modify: `ui/info_pane.go`
- Modify: `ui/info_pane_test.go`
- Modify: `app/app_state.go`

**Step 1: write the failing test**

Add tests to `ui/info_pane_test.go`:

```go
func TestInfoPane_PlanSummaryWithGoalAndLifecycle(t *testing.T) {
    pane := NewInfoPane()
    pane.SetSize(70, 40)
    now := time.Now()
    pane.SetData(InfoData{
        IsPlanHeaderSelected: true,
        PlanName:             "improved-info-tab",
        PlanStatus:           "implementing",
        PlanBranch:           "plan/improved-info-tab",
        PlanGoal:             "persist subtask statuses and redesign the info pane",
        PlanningAt:           now.Add(-2 * time.Hour),
        ImplementingAt:       now.Add(-1 * time.Hour),
        AllWaveSubtasks: []WaveSubtaskGroup{
            {WaveNumber: 1, Subtasks: []SubtaskDisplay{
                {Number: 1, Title: "schema migration", Status: "complete"},
                {Number: 2, Title: "store methods", Status: "complete"},
            }},
            {WaveNumber: 2, Subtasks: []SubtaskDisplay{
                {Number: 3, Title: "http endpoints", Status: "running"},
                {Number: 4, Title: "UI overhaul", Status: "pending"},
            }},
        },
        CompletedTasks: 2,
        TotalSubtasks:  4,
    })

    output := pane.String()
    assert.Contains(t, output, "persist subtask statuses")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "lifecycle")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "implementing")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "2/4")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "schema migration")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "✓")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "●")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "○")
}

func TestInfoPane_InstanceWithTaskAssignment(t *testing.T) {
    pane := NewInfoPane()
    pane.SetSize(70, 30)
    pane.SetData(InfoData{
        HasInstance:    true,
        HasPlan:        true,
        Title:          "my-feature-coder",
        Status:         "running",
        PlanName:       "my-feature",
        PlanGoal:       "add dark mode toggle",
        PlanStatus:     "implementing",
        AgentType:      "coder",
        TaskNumber:     3,
        TotalTasks:     6,
        TaskTitle:      "http endpoints",
    })

    output := pane.String()
    assert.Contains(t, output, "add dark mode toggle")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "task 3 of 6: http endpoints")
}
```

**Step 2: run test to verify it fails**

```bash
go test ./ui/... -run 'TestInfoPane_PlanSummaryWithGoalAndLifecycle|TestInfoPane_InstanceWithTaskAssignment' -v
```

expected: FAIL — `PlanGoal`, `AllWaveSubtasks`, `WaveSubtaskGroup`, `SubtaskDisplay`, etc. undefined

**Step 3: write minimal implementation**

**ui/info_pane.go** changes:

Add new types to `InfoData`:

```go
// New fields on InfoData:
PlanGoal       string
TaskTitle      string // task title for the instance's assigned task

// Phase timestamps (zero = not reached yet)
PlanningAt     time.Time
ImplementingAt time.Time
ReviewingAt    time.Time
DoneAt         time.Time

// All-waves subtask progress
AllWaveSubtasks []WaveSubtaskGroup
CompletedTasks  int
TotalSubtasks   int
```

New types:

```go
type SubtaskDisplay struct {
    Number int
    Title  string
    Status string // "pending", "running", "complete", "failed"
}

type WaveSubtaskGroup struct {
    WaveNumber int
    Subtasks   []SubtaskDisplay
}
```

Rewrite `renderPlanSummary()` to match the approved Mockup A:
1. **Plan metadata section** — name, status, branch, review cycle (existing, keep).
2. **Goal section** — new section header "goal" + divider + wrapped goal text.
3. **Lifecycle section** — new section header "lifecycle" + divider + phase rows with filled/unfilled bullet and timestamp or "—".
4. **Progress section** — header "progress" with inline fraction + ASCII bar + divider + per-wave groups with per-task rows showing glyph + number + title.
5. **Instances section** — existing instance counts.
6. **View plan doc button** — existing, keep at bottom.

Add helper methods:
- `renderGoalSection()` — renders goal text wrapped to pane width.
- `renderLifecycleSection()` — renders phase timeline with ● for reached phases (with timestamp) and ○ for unreached.
- `renderProgressSection()` — renders ASCII progress bar + all-waves task list with status glyphs (✓/●/✗/○).

For the **instance view**, modify `renderInstanceSection()`:
- After "plan name" row, add a "goal" row showing `PlanGoal` (one-liner, truncated to width).
- Change the "task" row from `"4 of 6"` to `"task 4 of 6: http endpoints"` using the new `TaskTitle` field.

**app/app_state.go** changes:

Modify `updateInfoPaneForPlanHeader()`:
- Load subtasks from store: `subtasks, err := m.taskStore.GetSubtasks(m.taskStoreProject, planFile)`.
- Group subtasks by wave number into `[]ui.WaveSubtaskGroup`.

parts.text: a and i agree with instance

The user confirmed Mockup A and agreed with the instance-selected view enhancement. Now I need to write the full implementation plan.

Let me think through the implementation carefully:

**Database changes needed:**
1. Add phase timestamp columns to `tasks` table: `planning_at`, `implementing_at`, `reviewing_at`, `done_at`
2. Add `subtasks` table: `id`, `project`, `plan_filename`, `task_number`, `wave_number`, `title`, `status`, `updated_at`

**Store interface changes:**
- New methods on `Store` interface for subtasks: `CreateSubtasks`, `GetSubtasks`, `UpdateSubtaskStatus`


- Phase timestamps can be handled through the existing `Update` method or a dedicated `SetPhaseTimestamp` method, with implementations needed for both SQLiteStore and HTTPStore

**Task parser:**
The existing wave and task parser just needs to feed data into the subtask rows when the plan is registered or its content changes.

**Info pane UI:**
I'm overhauling `renderPlanSummary()` to show the goal, lifecycle phases, and per-wave progress with individual task details, plus enhancing the instance view to display task assignments.

**App layer wiring:**
The WaveOrchestrator needs to trigger subtask status updates when tasks complete, and the plan registration flow needs to parse and populate the subtask table.

Now I'm structuring this into three waves: first the database schema and store layer with no UI dependencies, then the data pipeline to ingest subtasks and record phase transitions, and finally the UI layer that depends on having that data available.

For Wave 1, the SQLiteStore and HTTPStore implementations can happen in parallel since they're independent. Wave 2 combines subtask ingestion from plan content with phase timestamp recording and orchestrator state syncing, though I'm realizing some of these tasks have overlapping concerns that might be worth consolidating.

For Wave 3, I'm hitting a potential conflict—both the plan header info pane overhaul and instance-selected info pane enhancement modify the InfoData struct and info_pane.go file. Even though they touch different fields and rendering logic, working on the same files simultaneously could create merge issues. I need to decide whether to combine them into a single larger task or split them into sequential waves.

Looking at the time estimates, the plan header work is substantial at 30-40 minutes, while the instance view changes are minimal—just adding a couple of lines for goal and task assignment info. That's too small to stand alone, so merging them makes sense. I'll fold the instance enhancement into Task 5 as part of the same InfoData expansion, keeping them sequential but within a reasonable task scope.

For the store layer, I'm realizing the interface definition creates a hard dependency—Task 1 needs to define the Store interface and implement SQLiteStore, then Task 2 can build HTTPStore and Server endpoints on top of that. They can't truly run in parallel since Task 2 depends on knowing what methods Task 1 is adding to the interface. I should probably consolidate this into a single Wave 1 task that covers the schema, interface, and both store implementations together. I'm realizing the store layer needs to be split by actual dependencies rather than logical grouping. The HTTP layer depends on the Store interface being defined first, so those should be separate waves. The schema, interface, and SQLiteStore implementation form the foundation that everything else builds on, while the HTTP client and server endpoints can come after. The data pipeline and UI layers will follow once the store methods are available.

But now I'm noticing that both the ingestion pipeline and timestamp recording will modify `app_state.go`, so I need to check whether they're actually touching the same functions or if they can coexist in the same wave without conflicts.

Looking more carefully, Task 3 would update the `ingestTaskContent()` function to parse subtasks and handle plan registration, while Task 4 would hook into FSM transitions and the wave orchestrator to record timestamps. They're in different parts of the file, so they could theoretically run in parallel, but having two agents edit the same file concurrently is risky.

Let me reconsider the whole breakdown — I think I'm making this too complicated. The real constraint is that HTTPStore depends on the Store interface being defined first, so Task 1 and Task 2 can't actually be parallel.

So I'll consolidate: Wave 1 is the entire data layer — schema, Store interface, SQLiteStore implementation, HTTPStore, and all the server endpoints. It's cohesive work around 35-45 minutes, right at the edge but everything's tightly coupled so it shouldn't be split.

Wave 2 splits into two independent tasks: one for ingesting subtasks and recording phase timestamps across the FSM and orchestrator, and another for the UI overhaul. The UI task doesn't strictly need the data populated first — it can work with empty data initially.

But actually, the UI task includes the app layer wiring that connects the store to the InfoData struct and renders it. That means Task 3 depends on what Task 2 writes, so they need to stay in separate waves.

Let me reorganize this differently. I'm thinking about splitting Task 1 into smaller pieces since it's doing a lot — schema migrations, the store interface, and three different implementations. If I break that up, I could have:

Wave 1 with just the schema and SQLiteStore for subtasks and phase timestamps, then Wave 2 handles the data ingestion pipeline, and Wave 3 covers the UI overhaul. That's only three tasks though, and the scope suggests I should aim for more. Let me see if I can split this further.

Actually, I could separate the HTTP layer from the ingestion work. So Wave 2 becomes two tasks: one for HTTPStore and server endpoints, another for hooking the ingestion pipeline into the FSM and orchestrator. That gives me four tasks total, which feels more balanced for a Medium feature.

Looking at the time estimates, Task 1 is about 30 minutes for the schema and SQLiteStore, Task 2 is 25 minutes for HTTP and server work, Task 3 is 30 minutes for the ingestion and FSM integration, and Task 4 is 40 minutes for the UI redesign and app layer wiring. That's roughly 125 minutes total, which seems reasonable.

Now I'm wondering about the dependency between Task 3 and Task 2. The ingestion pipeline works through the Store interface, so it could technically use just SQLiteStore for testing. But the TUI runs against HTTPStore in production, so Task 3 really needs both the interface from Task 1 and the HTTP transport from Task 2 to be complete.

Looking at the actual code structure, the app layer uses the Store interface, which means tests can work with SQLiteStore alone. However, Task 2 and Task 3 don't overlap in files — Task 2 handles the HTTP layer while Task 3 covers the app state and orchestration logic — so they can run in parallel without conflicts.

Now I'm checking whether Task 3 and Task 4 can also be concurrent.  Both tasks modify `app/app_state.go`, so they'd create a file conflict and need to be sequenced separately.

This gives me the final wave structure: Task 1 stands alone in Wave 1 with the schema and store foundation, then Wave 2 runs Tasks 2 and 3 together since they touch different files, and Task 4 follows in its own wave due to the shared `app/app_state.go` modifications.

Let me verify there are no other overlaps between Task 2 and Task 3 — Task 2 handles the HTTP layer while Task 3 manages data ingestion and state transitions, so they're cleanly separated.

Now I'm working through the specifics of Task 1, starting with the database schema...

Defining the schema... I'm debating whether to store the goal separately or parse it from the content each time. Parsing on-the-fly seems cleaner since content is already in the database, but that means the info pane would parse repeatedly on every render. I think the better approach is to extract and cache `goal`, `architecture`, and `tech_stack` as columns when content is set, avoiding that repeated parsing overhead. Though actually, the parsing itself is pretty fast with regex on a small markdown file, and since `TaskState` already loads content into memory via `List()`, the rendering cost would be negligible—so maybe I'm overthinking this.

Wait, I need to check the actual code structure. Looking at it more carefully, `taskstore.TaskEntry` does include `Content`, but `taskstate.TaskEntry` doesn't, and `Load()` drops the content field when copying from store to state. So content isn't actually in the in-memory cache—it's fetched on-demand via `GetContent()`. For the info pane, I could either parse content asynchronously when needed, store the parsed metadata as DB columns for synchronous loading, or cache the parsed plan data in the app layer.

Actually, the wave orchestrator already does this caching—it has `m.waveOrchestrators[planFile]` with the parsed `Plan` containing `Goal`, `Architecture`, and `TechStack`. So that data's already available, I just need to pass it to the `InfoData` struct. For plans without an orchestrator, I could parse lazily when selected or add a `parsedPlans` cache in the app layer to avoid adding DB columns.

Since subtask creation requires parsing anyway, I'll store `goal`, `architecture`, and `tech_stack` as columns in the Tasks table. This avoids wasteful content loading for just three fields, keeps rendering synchronous, and extracts them during ingestion alongside subtask creation.

Adding schema columns... Defining the subtask status constants and the data structure to represent individual subtasks with their metadata, then adding the interface methods to store and retrieve subtasks from the database.

Now I'm refining the approach—`SetSubtasks` should do a full replacement since reparsing gives us the complete updated list, and `SetPlanMetadata` handles the three extracted fields separately. For `SetPhaseTimestamp`, a dedicated method makes sense because the FSM hook needs to update just the timestamp without touching other fields.

Let me step back and focus on what the info pane actually needs: the plan metadata and status are already there, the goal text needs to be stored somewhere, phase timestamps need persistence, and subtask data with statuses needs to be tracked. Most of this is either already available or can be handled through the existing structures without adding too many new columns.

For plans without an orchestrator, I could parse the goal on-demand, but that requires database access. The simplest approach is to store the goal as a column during content ingestion, add a separate table for subtasks, and track phase timestamps on the tasks table itself. I don't need to store architecture or tech stack details since the goal alone covers what the mockup requires.

Looking at the mockup again, it's really just asking for the goal text to be stored and displayed. Let me keep this minimal and only persist the goal for now.

Now I need to update the data structures and database layer. The `TaskEntry` structs in both taskstate and taskstore need the new fields, and I'll have to update the conversion functions and database scanning logic to handle them. It's mostly mechanical boilerplate, so I'll break this into focused tasks: first the schema and store interface with SQLite implementation, then the struct updates and database scanning functions. The info pane UI needs to display the goal, lifecycle phases with timestamps, and progress across all waves, so I'm updating the render function to show these sections along with a progress bar and enhancing the instance view to wire up the new data from the store. Before I write the full plan, let me check the FSM code to see where I should hook the timestamp tracking.
Good — Mockup A for plan header, plus task assignment info on instance view. Let me check a few more files to nail down the implementation details, then I'll write the plan.

<content>1: package taskfsm
2: 
3: import (
4: 	"fmt"
5: 
6: 	"github.com/kastheco/kasmos/config/taskstate"
7: 	"github.com/kastheco/kasmos/config/taskstore"
8: )
9: 
10: // Status represents the lifecycle state of a plan.
11: type Status string
12: 
13: const (
14: 	StatusReady        Status = "ready"
15: 	StatusPlanning     Status = "planning"
16: 	StatusImplementing Status = "implementing"
17: 	StatusReviewing    Status = "reviewing"
18: 	StatusDone         Status = "done"
19: 	StatusCancelled    Status = "cancelled"
20: )
21: 
22: // Event represents a lifecycle transition trigger.
23: type Event string
24: 
25: const (
26: 	PlanStart              Event = "plan_start"
27: 	PlannerFinished        Event = "planner_finished"
28: 	ImplementStart         Event = "implement_start"
29: 	ImplementFinished      Event = "implement_finished"
30: 	ReviewApproved         Event = "review_approved"
31: 	ReviewChangesRequested Event = "review_changes_requested"
32: 	RequestReview          Event = "request_review"
33: 	StartOver              Event = "start_over"
34: 	Reimplement            Event = "reimplement"
35: 	Cancel                 Event = "cancel"
36: 	Reopen                 Event = "reopen"
37: )
38: 
39: // IsUserOnly returns true if this event can only be triggered from the TUI,
40: // never by agent sentinel files.
41: func (e Event) IsUserOnly() bool {
42: 	switch e {
43: 	case StartOver, Reimplement, RequestReview, Cancel, Reopen:
44: 		return true
45: 	}
46: 	return false
47: }
48: 
49: // transitionTable defines all valid state transitions.
50: // Key: current status → event → new status.
51: var transitionTable = map[Status]map[Event]Status{
52: 	StatusReady: {
53: 		PlanStart:      StatusPlanning,
54: 		ImplementStart: StatusImplementing,
55: 		Cancel:         StatusCancelled,
56: 	},
57: 	StatusPlanning: {
58: 		PlanStart:       StatusPlanning, // allow restart after crash/interrupt
59: 		PlannerFinished: StatusReady,
60: 		Cancel:          StatusCancelled,
61: 	},
62: 	StatusImplementing: {
63: 		ImplementFinished: StatusReviewing,
64: 		Cancel:            StatusCancelled,
65: 	},
66: 	StatusReviewing: {
67: 		ReviewApproved:         StatusDone,
68: 		ReviewChangesRequested: StatusImplementing,
69: 		Cancel:                 StatusCancelled,
70: 	},
71: 	StatusDone: {
72: 		StartOver:     StatusPlanning,
73: 		Reimplement:   StatusImplementing, // resume implementation without resetting branch
74: 		RequestReview: StatusReviewing,    // retrigger review for unmerged branches
75: 		Cancel:        StatusCancelled,    // explicit user cancellation from done
76: 	},
77: 	StatusCancelled: {
78: 		Reopen: StatusPlanning,
79: 	},
80: }
81: 
82: // ApplyTransition returns the new status for the given current status and event.
83: // Returns an error if the transition is not valid.
84: func ApplyTransition(current Status, event Event) (Status, error) {
85: 	events, ok := transitionTable[current]
86: 	if !ok {
87: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("no transitions defined for status %q", current)
88: 	}
89: 	next, ok := events[event]
90: 	if !ok {
91: 		return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid transition: %q + %q", current, event)
92: 	}
93: 	return next, nil
94: }
95: 
96: // TaskStateMachine is the sole writer of plan state. All plan status mutations
97: // must flow through Transition(). The store handles concurrency via SQLite.
98: type TaskStateMachine struct {
99: 	dir     string          // docs/plans/ directory (for file operations)
100: 	store   taskstore.Store // always non-nil
101: 	project string          // project name used with the store
102: }
103: 
104: // New creates a TaskStateMachine backed by the given store.
105: func New(store taskstore.Store, project, dir string) *TaskStateMachine {
106: 	return &TaskStateMachine{dir: dir, store: store, project: project}
107: }
108: 
109: // Transition applies an event to a plan's current status. It reads the current
110: // state from the store, validates the transition, writes the new state, and returns.
111: // Concurrency is handled server-side via SQLite's own locking.
112: func (m *TaskStateMachine) Transition(planFile string, event Event) error {
113: 	ps, err := taskstate.Load(m.store, m.project, m.dir)
114: 	if err != nil {
115: 		return fmt.Errorf("load plan state: %w", err)
116: 	}
117: 	entry, ok := ps.Entry(planFile)
118: 	if !ok {
119: 		return fmt.Errorf("plan not found: %s", planFile)
120: 	}
121: 	currentStatus := mapLegacyStatus(entry.Status)
122: 	newStatus, err := ApplyTransition(currentStatus, event)
123: 	if err != nil {
124: 		return err
125: 	}
126: 	// ForceSetStatus writes through to the store.
127: 	return ps.ForceSetStatus(planFile, taskstate.Status(newStatus))
128: }
129: 
130: // mapLegacyStatus converts old planstate statuses to FSM statuses.
131: // Handles the consolidated aliases (in_progress → implementing, completed/finished → done).
132: func mapLegacyStatus(s taskstate.Status) Status {
133: 	switch s {
134: 	case "in_progress":
135: 		return StatusImplementing
136: 	case "completed", "finished":
137: 		return StatusDone
138: 	default:
139: 		return Status(s)
140: 	}
141: }

(End of file - total 141 lines)
</content>
